title: Lamentation of Mary to Saint Bernard
summary: The repository XML represents University of Michigan CME item CME00007, a keyed encoding of Gustav Kribel's 1885 parallel edition. Kribel transcribed two Middle English witnesses: the Vernon Manuscript, Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 287rc-288vc, and Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.1, ff. 21r-29v. His Latin page-foot comparison is an editorial source layer, not a third Middle English witness. The current DIMEV census records four additional witnesses that are not ancestors of CME00007: Bodleian MSS Laud Misc. 463 and Douce 126, Trinity College Oxford MS 57, and British Library Add MS 11307. A complete 710-canvas color surrogate of the Vernon Manuscript is cached, with the work mapped to canvases 460-463 inside the full codex. No complete public digital facsimile was found for the other five witnesses; only unrelated selected surfaces are public for Laud Misc. 463 and Douce 126, and institutional request routes are recorded separately.
review_notes: The source graph distinguishes the anonymous Middle English work, six physical witnesses, nineteenth-century editions, modern reproductions, U-M's encoding, and this repository artifact.

Only the Vernon and Cambridge Dd.1.1 witness texts feed Kribel's 1885 edition and therefore CME00007. The four witnesses added by the modern DIMEV census are collateral witnesses, not direct ancestors of the encoded text.

The complete Vernon digital object was cached rather than only the four target canvases. The exact work portion is ff. 287rc-288vc, IIIF canvases 460-463; the cached 1600-pixel bundle is complete in surface coverage, not a preservation master.

The public Digital Bodleian records for Laud Misc. 463 and Douce 126 contain only one selected surface each, neither carrying this poem. Their cached manifests are metadata-only supporting records and must not be presented as work facsimiles.

Rights for the medieval text, editorial matter, reprints, digital scans, manuscript photography, datasets, and requested future images are separate layers and are not inherited through lineage relations.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00007
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The XML's IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID values all identify CME00007.

The source XML contains two Middle English DIV2 witness streams followed by Kribel's Latin comparison as a third DIV2; the wrapper label versions of text is therefore edition-structural rather than a census of three medieval witnesses.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00007
entities.description: A publicly viewable electronic text of the textual portion of Kribel's article. It serializes the printed A and B columns as consecutive Middle English divisions, then serializes the Latin page-foot comparison as a further division, while preserving printed-page milestones, notes, and italic expansions.
notes: The U-M collection page describes its historical editions as manually keyed with in-house proofing and editing.
The item display lists Richard Rolle among authors because it reproduces edition-level legacy metadata; this is not accepted work-level authorship evidence.
A 2016 revision record says the item was checked, restructured, and validated; it does not establish the date of the original electronic publication.

entities.label: Kribel's 1885 parallel edition
alternate_labels: Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae
entities.description: The immediate print source of CME00007: a German study followed by a parallel, non-normalized transcription of Vernon (A) and CUL Dd.1.1 (B), with a Latin pseudo-Bernard comparison at the foot of the pages and editorial notes. The article introduction occupies pp. 67-84; the portion encoded by U-M occupies pp. 85-114.

bibliographic.citation: Gustav Kribel, 'Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae,' Englische Studien 8 (1885), 67-114.
bibliographic.title: Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae

notes: Kribel says this article revises the partial form printed in his 1883 Breslau dissertation.
He rejects the Cambridge catalogue's Richard Rolle conjecture on dialectal grounds and concludes that the author cannot be identified.
His Latin comparison was made from an Antwerp 1616 printing; its exact bibliographic identity remains unresolved and it must not be treated as the medieval translator's physical exemplar.

entities.label: Johnson Reprint Corporation photographic reprint of Englische Studien 8
entities.description: A 1965 photographic reprint of the 1885 journal volume. The Internet Archive scan used here images this reprint copy rather than an original 1885 printing.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of the 1965 Englische Studien 8 reprint
entities.description: A complete 522-page PDF scan of the 1965 photographic reprint. Kribel's article is at PDF pages 77-124; the exact portion represented by CME00007 is printed pp. 85-114, PDF pages 95-124.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1
entities.description: One of Kribel's two directly transcribed witnesses. The poem occupies ff. 287rc-288vc in the main codex and is complete in 736 lines in Kribel's transcription.

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian provides a complete 710-canvas color surrogate.

physical_description.extent: viii + 342 + i surviving leaves, incomplete; originally viii + 413. The poem occupies ff. 287rc-288vc.
physical_description.format: Large religious and didactic miscellany; the target surfaces use three columns
notes: The Bodleian record gives England, West Midlands? as origin.
The work begins in column c of fol. 287r and ends in column c of fol. 288v; the last surface also begins the next item.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian facsimile of the Vernon Manuscript
entities.description: A complete color digital surrogate of the physical codex, including covers, flyleaves, all surviving manuscript leaves, and the four canvases carrying this poem.
entities.reproduction_format: 710 color IIIF canvases; locally bundled as 1600-pixel-wide JPEG requests plus manifest and source inventory
notes: The cached bundle covers the complete Digital Bodleian physical object, not only ff. 287r-288v.
The work portion is mapped to canvases 460-463, with stable start and end canvas URLs recorded in access metadata.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.1
entities.description: Kribel's second directly transcribed witness. The poem occupies ff. 21r-29v, corresponding to manuscript pages 29-42 in the 1856 catalogue; fol. 26 was already missing, and the surviving text is imperfect.

date_statements.label: catalogue dating
date_statements.value: latter half of the fourteenth century

date_statements.label: Kribel's dating
date_statements.notes: Retained separately because it conflicts with the older CUL catalogue dating.

holding.notes: No public complete digital facsimile was found. CUL Imaging Services accepts image orders when exact shelfmark and folios are supplied.

physical_description.extent: Originally paginated to 552; now mutilated. The poem occupies ff. 21r-29v / manuscript pp. 29-42, with fol. 26 missing.
physical_description.format: Miscellany, averaging about 50 lines per page
notes: The 1856 catalogue calls the codex mutilated and gives the poem's rubric, incipit, explicit, and page span.
DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 558, Isle of Ely.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 463
entities.description: A collateral witness recorded by DIMEV, not used in Kribel's edition. The poem is at ff. 160ra-164rb, with its title at f. 159v.

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian exposes only a selected image of f. 115r, outside the poem; complete imaging may be requested from Bodleian Imaging Services.

physical_description.extent: The poem occupies ff. 160ra-164rb; its title is at f. 159v.
notes: DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 7750, Worcestershire.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian selected image for MS. Laud Misc. 463
entities.description: A one-surface Digital Bodleian object derived from older 35mm material. It shows f. 115r, not the target poem at ff. 160ra-164rb, and is not a complete manuscript facsimile.
entities.reproduction_format: one selected digital image; incomplete and outside the work span

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Douce 126
entities.description: A collateral witness recorded by DIMEV, not used in Kribel's edition. The poem occupies ff. 84v-90.

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.value: first half of the fifteenth century

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian exposes only a selected image of ff. 68v-69r, outside the poem; complete imaging may be requested from Bodleian Imaging Services.

notes: DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 6220, Essex.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian selected image for MS. Douce 126
entities.description: A one-surface Digital Bodleian object derived from older 35mm material. It shows ff. 68v-69r, not the target poem at ff. 84v-90, and is not a complete manuscript facsimile.
entities.reproduction_format: one selected digital image; incomplete and outside the work span

entities.label: Trinity College Oxford MS 57
entities.description: A collateral witness recorded by DIMEV and later collated by Horstmann. The poem is the final item, ff. 167r-169v, and ends imperfectly at the end of the codex; the modern catalogue estimates that about five pages are missing.

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.value: last quarter of the fourteenth century, after 1375

holding.notes: The Trinity manuscripts are administered through the college's special-collections arrangements at the Bodleian Weston Library. No public complete facsimile was found.

physical_description.extent: 169 leaves plus flyleaves; incomplete at beginning and end. The poem occupies ff. 167r-169v.
notes: The catalogue places production in southern England.
DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 5850, Sussex.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 11307
entities.description: A collateral fifteenth-century vellum witness recorded by DIMEV, at ff. 97v-117v, with facing modern transcripts by Joseph Haslewood. It was not used in Kribel's edition.

holding.notes: The current catalog record has no Digitised Content link. The BL digitisation service accepts quotation requests with exact shelfmark and folio details.

notes: DIMEV associates this witness with LALME linguistic profile 7890, Warwickshire.
The BL catalogue describes a four-line-stanza dialogue; that description and DIMEV's general eight-line abababab work-level analysis should be retained at their respective descriptive layers.

entities.label: Horstmann's 1892 Vernon edition
entities.description: A later edition based on the Vernon Manuscript, with variants from CUL Dd.1.1, Trinity College Oxford MS 57, and an unidentified source called MS. L. 70. It is a comparison source, not an ancestor of CME00007.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Minor Poems of the Vernon MS, part 1, EETS Original Series 98 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1892), 297-328.
bibliographic.title: The Minor Poems of the Vernon MS, Part I
bibliographic.container_title: Early English Text Society, Original Series

notes: Horstmann attributes the poem to Richard Maidstone without giving reasoning accepted by the current DIMEV record.
The identity of MS. L. 70 was not established and it does not appear in DIMEV's current six-witness census.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of The Minor Poems of the Vernon MS, part 1
entities.description: A complete 473-page PDF scan of the 1892 volume. The poem occupies printed pp. 297-328, PDF pages 318-349.

entities.label: Unidentified manuscript designated MS. L. 70 by Horstmann
entities.description: A witness siglum used in Horstmann's 1892 apparatus. Its institution, current shelfmark, extent, survival, and relationship to DIMEV's six known witnesses have not been established.

entities.label: Unresolved Antwerp 1616 Latin pseudo-Bernard printing
entities.description: The early-modern Latin printing that Kribel says he used for his page-foot comparison. The present research did not establish an exact title, printer, or surviving digital copy, and no later Antwerp edition has been substituted for it.

date_statements.label: publication
date_statements.notes: The place and year are Kribel's explicit statement; the exact bibliographic object remains unidentified.

entities.label: CUL manuscript catalogue, volume 1 (1856)
entities.description: The historical institutional catalogue containing the Dd.1.1 description on pp. 1-6, with the poem entry on p. 2.

bibliographic.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. 1 (Cambridge: University Press, 1856), 1-6.
bibliographic.title: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge

entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse 2.0-alpha.1, record-3066
entities.description: The current six-witness census used to supplement Kribel's two-witness knowledge and to keep related DIMEV records distinct.

bibliographic.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation, version 2.0-alpha.1 (2026), record-3066, doi:10.5281/zenodo.20850921.
bibliographic.title: The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation

entities.label: Snow's 2012 scholarly study of the Lamentation tradition
entities.description: A modern thesis used as a supporting scholarly description of the six-witness census, affective-devotional context, and proposed Anglo-Norman intermediary. It is not an ancestor of CME00007.

bibliographic.citation: Clare Marie Snow, Maria Mediatrix: Mediating the Divine in the Devotional Literature of Late Medieval and Early Modern England (PhD thesis, University of Toronto, 2012).
bibliographic.title: Maria Mediatrix: Mediating the Divine in the Devotional Literature of Late Medieval and Early Modern England

access.access_method: Submit an order through CUL Imaging Services, specifying MS Dd.1.1 and requesting the complete physical codex; identify ff. 21r-29v as the work portion.
access.contact: Use the CUL Imaging Services online order form.
notes: CUL's terms distinguish private research supply from publication and restrict sharing or uploading supplied private copies without separate permission.
Ask for the whole manuscript first; the target span for association is ff. 21r-29v, with fol. 26 missing.

notes: The published minimum charge is £20, but a full-codex request may be substantially more expensive and must be quoted.

notes: A later Antwerp Opera Omnia was found during research but was not substituted for Kribel's explicitly dated 1616 source.

rights.component: underlying anonymous Middle English work text, considered independently of any manuscript photograph, transcription, edition, or encoding
rights.basis: The work and all known manuscript attestations predate modern copyright by centuries. This assessment does not extend to photographs, editorial matter, transcriptions, database structure, or provider terms.

rights.component: U-M electronic text and item content as supplied by the provider
rights.basis: The U-M item and collection pages label the material public domain and apply the No Copyright - United States rights statement.
notes: This records U-M's provider assertion; it does not grant rights in independent manuscript photographs or modern scholarship linked from this manifest.

rights.component: textual content of the local CME00007 XML snapshot
rights.basis: The artifact represents the U-M item whose provider asserts No Copyright - United States; no separate rights statement was found in the local wrapper.

rights.component: Kribel's 1885 transcription, introduction, notes, and editorial arrangement
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1885 and is in the public domain in the United States by age.

rights.component: 1965 photographic reprint edition and any new front matter
rights.basis: The scanned object is a 1965 reprint rather than an 1885 original. No renewal, registration, or reprint-rights investigation was completed, and the public-domain status of the underlying article does not decide rights in new 1965 matter.

rights.component: Internet Archive digital scan files and scan-specific metadata
rights.basis: The item metadata did not expose an item-level license or rights field. Rights in the underlying 1885 article, the 1965 reprint layer, and the digital scan are therefore recorded separately.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and IIIF image derivatives
rights.basis: The Digital Bodleian manifest attributes the images to the Bodleian Libraries and supplies a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 license; Bodleian reproduction guidance governs publication uses.
notes: The license allows reuse only within its terms; commercial repository or print-on-demand uses require separate review or permission.
Local caching for research does not itself authorize redistribution of the cached ZIP.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian selected photograph and IIIF metadata
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest attributes the image to the Bodleian Libraries and supplies a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 license.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian selected photograph and IIIF metadata
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest attributes the image to the Bodleian Libraries and supplies a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 license.

rights.component: Horstmann's 1892 transcription, variants, notes, and editorial arrangement
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1892 and is in the public domain in the United States by age.

rights.component: Google-digitized and Internet Archive-hosted scan files and scan-specific metadata
rights.basis: The underlying 1892 edition is public domain, but the PDF embeds a Google public-domain notice requesting attribution and noncommercial use, while the Internet Archive item exposes no item-level license. These provider and scan layers are not treated as equivalent to the edition's public-domain status.
The catalogue was published in 1856 and is in the public domain in the United States by age.
The Zenodo release metadata applies Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 to the dataset.
A modern thesis remains protected absent a broader license; public access through Library and Archives Canada does not place it in the public domain or authorize substantial republication.
notes: Facts and properly cited scholarly conclusions may be used in research; copying protected expression or redistributing the cached PDF is a separate question.

editorial_practices.source_feature: letters or syllables supplied when expanding manuscript abbreviations
editorial_practices.representation: italic type in the 1885 edition; HI elements in CME00007
editorial_practices.description: Kribel says he marks expanded letters and syllables in italics. U-M preserves those italics structurally, so they can be researched as editorial expansion signals but must not be assumed to reproduce the manuscripts' original abbreviation marks.
editorial_practices.scope: Both Middle English parallel witness transcriptions on printed pp. 85-114.

editorial_practices.representation: Kribel states that the two Middle English texts were not normalized
editorial_practices.description: Kribel presents the parallel witnesses in their differing spellings rather than reducing them to a normalized copy text. His punctuation, type, expansions, and layout remain editorial layers despite that policy.
editorial_practices.scope: Witnesses A and B on printed pp. 85-114.

editorial_practices.source_feature: two materially variant Middle English witnesses and selected Latin parallels
editorial_practices.representation: Vernon A and Cambridge B in parallel columns with Latin comparison below and notes keyed to the pages
editorial_practices.description: The edition aligns two independent witness texts stanza by stanza while retaining additions, omissions, and reordering, then supplies selected Latin source parallels. The layout is an editorial comparison, not a single composite medieval text.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Kribel's complete article on printed pp. 67-114
editorial_practices.representation: only the edited Middle English streams, Latin comparison, and associated notes on pp. 85-114 are encoded
editorial_practices.description: U-M omits Kribel's German study on pp. 67-84. Consequently, CME00007 is not a complete digital transcription of the article even though it includes the complete parallel text section.
editorial_practices.scope: Entire U-M item relative to the source article.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Kribel's simultaneous A/B parallel columns and page-foot Latin comparison
editorial_practices.representation: three consecutive DIV2 streams under a DIV1 labeled versions of text
editorial_practices.description: The encoding serializes the printed visual comparison into A, then B, then Latin divisions. This preserves the content but not the page-level simultaneity and can misleadingly resemble three coordinate medieval versions unless the edition structure is consulted.
editorial_practices.scope: TEXT/BODY/DIV1 and its three DIV2 children.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Vernon base text and variants in CUL Dd.1.1, Trinity 57, and unresolved MS. L. 70
editorial_practices.representation: Vernon text with a variant apparatus
editorial_practices.description: Horstmann's edition is useful for comparison with additional witnesses but does not replace Kribel as the source of CME00007. Its unsupported Richard Maidstone attribution is an assertion of that edition, not an editorial fact to inherit.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME00007.xml.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD/REVDESCR; EEBO/IDG; TEXT/BODY/DIV1
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00007 and preserves Kribel's A and B Middle English witness streams, Latin comparison, notes, page milestones, italic expansions, and a 2016 restructuring review. Element inspection finds 91 LG/736 L in A and 96 LG/764 L in B.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00007.
evidence.locator: item title, responsibility display, rights, contents, and entire-text link
evidence.summary: Identifies the item as Kribel's 1885 study, exposes the three serialized textual divisions, and labels the content public domain / No Copyright - United States. The displayed Richard Rolle authorship is legacy edition-level metadata and is contradicted by Kribel's argument and the current DIMEV record.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection information.
evidence.locator: collection description, production description, rights statement, and bulk XML information
evidence.summary: States that the collection consists largely of public-domain editions that were manually keyed, proofed and edited in house, and encoded in TEI; applies public-domain / No Copyright - United States language at collection level.

evidence.citation: Gustav Kribel, 'Studien zu Richard Rolle de Hampole. II. Lamentatio St. Bernardi de compassione Mariae,' Englische Studien 8 (1885), 67-114.
evidence.locator: article pp. 67-114; edited parallel texts and Latin comparison pp. 85-114; PDF pp. 77-124
evidence.summary: Immediate source edition. It identifies Vernon and CUL Dd.1.1, explains its parallel non-normalized transcription and italic expansions, compares the two witnesses and Latin sources, rejects Rolle authorship, and names an Antwerp 1616 Latin edition used for comparison.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive item englische-studien_1885_8, complete scan of Johnson Reprint Corporation's 1965 photographic reprint of Englische Studien 8 (1885).
evidence.locator: reprint title and copyright leaves; Kribel article PDF pp. 77-124
evidence.summary: Direct inspection establishes that the full scanned object is a 1965 photographic reprint, not an original 1885 physical copy; the item exposes no item-level license field.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1.
evidence.locator: whole-codex date, origin, material, extent, dimensions, and digital-object link
evidence.summary: Dates the Vernon Manuscript to 1381-1400, thought c. 1390, locates it tentatively in the West Midlands, and describes the complete surviving parchment codex.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript), complete Digital Bodleian IIIF facsimile.
evidence.locator: 710 canvases from upper cover through lower cover and spine; work canvases 460-463
evidence.summary: The official IIIF manifest exposes a complete 710-canvas color surrogate and image-license metadata. Its complete local bundle includes every public canvas and preserves exact source URLs.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of Digital Bodleian Vernon Manuscript canvases 460-463.
evidence.locator: ff. 287r-288v, specifically columns 287rc through 288vc
evidence.summary: Confirms the rubric and opening in column c of fol. 287r and the ending in column c of fol. 288v; the final surface also begins the following work. The manuscript uses three columns, rubrication, decorated initials, and visible scribal abbreviation marks not reproduced graphically by Kribel's italic expansions.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries Imaging Services, Order images.
evidence.locator: special-collections ordering route, contact, and minimum charge
evidence.summary: Provides the route for requesting manuscript photography, gives specialcollections.enquiries@bodleian.ox.ac.uk, and publishes a £20 minimum charge; full-codex requests require an individual quotation.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries Imaging Services, Copyright and reproducing images.
evidence.locator: noncommercial and academic reuse guidance; publication permission
evidence.summary: Distinguishes noncommercial or academic reuse under applicable terms from publication and other licensed uses. The IIIF manifests separately state CC BY-NC 4.0 for the public Bodleian images.

evidence.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. 1 (Cambridge: University Press, 1856), 1-6, especially p. 2.
evidence.locator: Dd.1.1 entry pp. 1-6; poem at printed p. 2 / PDF p. 24; manuscript pp. 29-42
evidence.summary: Describes Dd.1.1 as a mutilated, principally parchment codex of the latter half of the fourteenth century and gives the poem's rubric, incipit, explicit, manuscript-page range, Latin-source claim, and historical Rolle conjecture.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Imaging Services and Ordering Images.
evidence.locator: online image-ordering route and instructions
evidence.summary: Provides the institutional route for ordering digital images by shelfmark and folio and indicates that fees are quoted for supplied reproductions.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Terms and conditions for image supply and reproduction.
evidence.locator: private research supply, sharing restrictions, and publication permission
evidence.summary: States that images supplied for private research are not to be shared or uploaded and that publication and reproduction require separate permission and may incur fees.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Digital Bodleian selected-image object for MS. Laud Misc. 463.
evidence.locator: one selected surface, f. 115r; exact-shelfmark API completeness partial
evidence.summary: The public object contains only an unrelated selected surface and not the poem at ff. 160ra-164rb. Its manifest supplies official shelfmark, late-fourteenth-century date, parchment support, and CC BY-NC 4.0 image terms.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Digital Bodleian selected-image object for MS. Douce 126.
evidence.locator: one selected surface, ff. 68v-69r; exact-shelfmark API completeness partial
evidence.summary: The public object contains only an unrelated selected surface and not the poem at ff. 84v-90. Its manifest supplies official shelfmark, first-half fifteenth-century date, parchment support, and CC BY-NC 4.0 image terms.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation, version 2.0-alpha.1, record-3066 (2026).
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-3066 and associated data/Manuscripts.xml entries
evidence.summary: Supplies the current six-witness census, exact folio ranges, incipits, explicits, rubrics, eight-line abababab form, IMEV/NIMEV 1869, MED BIB857, LALME references, related records 1275 and 5020, and CC BY 4.0 release terms.

evidence.citation: Richard Gameson, The Medieval Manuscripts of Trinity College, Oxford: A Descriptive Catalogue (2018), MS 57.
evidence.locator: MS 57 description; final item at ff. 167r-169v
evidence.summary: Dates the southern-English parchment codex to the last quarter of the fourteenth century after 1375, describes its 169 leaves and contents, and identifies the final Lamentacio Marie et Bernardi as ending imperfectly at the codex boundary, about five pages short.
notes: The exact PDF was indexed and readable through browser search, but an automated direct download encountered a JavaScript proof-of-work challenge; no local copy is claimed.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 11307, record 032-002109064.
evidence.locator: whole-record title, scope and content, date, access, and blank Digitised Content field
evidence.summary: Describes a fifteenth-century vellum manuscript containing a dialogue between Saint Bernard and the Virgin with facing recent transcripts by Joseph Haslewood; the current record exposes no digitized-content link.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation services.
evidence.locator: quotation form requirements, manuscript photography, color options, and licensing contact
evidence.summary: Accepts digitisation quotation requests with exact shelfmark and page or folio ranges; explains that original manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned and that publication licensing is a separate step.

evidence.citation: British Library, Imaging Services terms and conditions.
evidence.locator: image supply, permitted use, licensing, and customer obligations
evidence.summary: Sets contractual terms for supplied images and confirms that image delivery and publication authorization are distinct.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Minor Poems of the Vernon MS, part 1, EETS Original Series 98 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1892), 297-328.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 297-328; PDF pp. 318-349
evidence.summary: A later Vernon-based edition with variants from CUL Dd.1.1, incomplete Trinity College Oxford MS 57, and unresolved MS. L. 70. It asserts Richard Maidstone authorship without supplying an argument accepted by the current DIMEV record. The complete scan contains a Google public-domain notice but no Internet Archive item-level license.

evidence.citation: Clare Marie Snow, Maria Mediatrix: Mediating the Divine in the Devotional Literature of Late Medieval and Early Modern England (PhD thesis, University of Toronto, 2012).
evidence.locator: chapter 3, especially thesis pp. 131-152 and p. 134 witness/source discussion
evidence.summary: Places the poem in affective Marian devotion, lists the same six IMEV 1869 witnesses, and, following C. W. Marx, reports an Anglo-Norman poetic Quis dabit intermediary and an added prologue in the Vernon form.

open_questions.question: Can complete color digital facsimiles of Bodleian MSS Laud Misc. 463 and Douce 126 be obtained and licensed for the project's intended reference use?
next_steps: Request quotations for the complete physical codices, not only the target leaves.
For Laud Misc. 463 identify ff. 159v-164rb as the title-and-work span; for Douce 126 identify ff. 84v-90.
Ask explicitly about repository redistribution, noncommercial reference display, and commercial print-on-demand research context before publishing any supplied images.
notes: The currently public one-image objects are outside both work spans and are not substitutes for complete facsimiles.

open_questions.question: Can complete color digital facsimiles be obtained for CUL MS Dd.1.1, Trinity College Oxford MS 57, and BL Add MS 11307?
next_steps: Ask each holding institution first for complete-codex photography and a quotation.
Associate the work respectively with Dd.1.1 ff. 21r-29v, Trinity 57 ff. 167r-169v, and Add MS 11307 ff. 97v-117v.
Record any supply contract and publication permission separately from copyright in the underlying medieval text.

open_questions.question: What physical manuscript did Horstmann mean by MS. L. 70?
next_steps: Trace Horstmann's source notes, sigla in adjacent EETS editions, and contemporary library catalogues.
Do not add L. 70 to the current six-witness census until it is identified or shown to duplicate a known witness.

open_questions.question: Which exact Antwerp 1616 pseudo-Bernard edition supplied Kribel's Latin comparison?
next_steps: Search historical Bernardine bibliographies and Antwerp printer catalogues for a 1616 edition containing the relevant Quis dabit text.
Compare title-page and passage evidence before linking any candidate; do not substitute the discoverable 1620 Antwerp Opera Omnia merely because it is similar.

open_questions.question: What rights, if any, subsist in Johnson Reprint Corporation's 1965 photographic reprint and its new matter?
next_steps: Inspect the reprint's copyright notice and determine whether a relevant U.S. renewal or registration exists.
Prefer an original 1885 scan for redistribution if one becomes available, while retaining this full scan for private source comparison.

open_questions.question: What exact Latin and/or Anglo-Norman textual route underlies the Middle English Lamentation?
next_steps: Consult C. W. Marx's full studies and subsequent work on the Quis dabit tradition.
Compare all six Middle English witnesses against the proposed Anglo-Norman intermediary and Latin pseudo-Bernard tradition.
Keep Kribel's early-modern comparison edition distinct from any inferred medieval exemplar.
